7: A revelation of God: 'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him.' Set in the context of Nineveh's coming destruction, this verse identifies the flip-side of divine wrath: the same YHWH who destroys his enemies is the stronghold for those who trust him. The NT's form of this double-truth: 'It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God' (Hebrews 10:31) for those who reject Christ; but 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1) for those who are in him. The refuge (maoz, stronghold/fortress) that Nahum declares is the God who is also Christ: 'the name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous man runs into it and is safe' (Proverbs 18:10).
1: A theme: 'The oracle concerning Nineveh. The book of the vision of Nahum of Elkosh.' Nahum opens by naming Nineveh — the capital of Assyria, the dominant imperial power of the ancient Near East, destroyer of Samaria (722 BCE). Nahum's oracle against the great empire is the OT pattern that runs to its fullest expression in Revelation's oracles against “Babylon” (Rome / the world-city, Rev 17-18). Christ is the divine judge who executes the verdict: 'He will judge the world in righteousness' (Acts 17:31); 'From his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations' (Revelation 19:15). Every oracle against every empire points to the one who will render final judgment on all anti-God power.
2: A revelation of God: 'The LORD is a jealous and avenging God; the LORD is avenging and wrathful; the LORD takes vengeance on his adversaries and keeps wrath for his enemies.' Nahum's opening description of YHWH draws on the covenant character of Exodus 34:14 (jealous God) and Deuteronomy 32:35 (vengeance is mine). The NT does not soften this: “Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord” (Romans 12:19, citing Deuteronomy 32:35); “It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31). The cross does not eliminate divine wrath — it satisfies it. Christ absorbed the vengeance that belongs to God against sin (1 Peter 2:24; Romans 3:25), so that those in him are no longer under it. The jealous, avenging God of Nahum 1:2 is the same God who “did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all” (Romans 8:32).
3: A revelation of God: 'The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, and the LORD will by no means clear the guilty.' Nahum cites the Exodus 34:6-7 divine character formula: merciful and slow to anger (Nahum 1:6) but not leaving the guilty unpunished (Nahum 1:7). Jonah announced repentance and the slow-anger quality prevailed; Nahum announces exhausted patience and the not-clearing-the-guilty quality prevails. The cross is where both attributes are satisfied simultaneously: God is “just and the justifier” (Romans 3:26) — just, because the guilt is not cleared without punishment; justifier, because Christ receives the punishment so the guilty can be declared righteous. Paul calls the cross the demonstration that God did not permanently overlook “previously committed sins” (Romans 3:25) — Nahum's patience-exhausted theme is exactly this: the slow anger eventually acts.
4: A revelation of God: 'He rebukes the sea and makes it dry; he dries up all the rivers; Bashan and Carmel wither; the bloom of Lebanon withers.' The theophanic imagery — YHWH's rebuke drying seas and withering mountains — echoes the Exodus (Exodus 14:21; Psalms 106:9) and the creation (God separating the waters, Genesis 1:9-10). Jesus rebukes the sea with the same authority: “He rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still” (Mark 4:39). The disciples' response — “Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?” — is the NT recognition that the One who rebukes the sea in Nahum and Jesus is the same divine person. Christ's miracles over nature are not departures from the OT portrait of YHWH but its fulfilments in incarnate form.
5: A revelation of God: 'The mountains quake before him; the hills melt; the earth heaves before him, the world and all who dwell in it.' The divine theophany that dismantles the physical creation anticipates the NT's eschatological trembling before the returning Christ: “Every mountain and island was removed from its place. Then the kings of the earth and the great ones ... hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains, calling to the mountains and rocks, Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb” (Revelation 6:14-16). The wrath of the Lamb — a paradoxical phrase — is the NT's most striking formulation of this Nahum-theophany: the gentle Christ is also the divine judge before whom creation trembles.
6: A revelation of God: 'Who can stand before his indignation? Who can endure the heat of his anger? His wrath is poured out like fire, and the rocks are broken into pieces by him.' Nahum's rhetorical question — who can stand before YHWH's wrath? — is echoed in Revelation's great judgment scene (Revelation 6:17: “for the great day of their wrath has come, and who can stand?”). Paul's answer to this question is the gospel: “Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? ... Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised” (Romans 8:33-34). The one who can stand is the one who stands in Christ — the only person against whom no charge holds, because his death paid every charge. Nahum's terrifying question finds its answer not in human strength but in Christ's substitutionary work.
8: A shadow: 'But with an overflowing flood he will make a complete end of the adversaries, and will pursue his enemies into darkness.' The flood-imagery of final judgment — YHWH overwhelming his enemies as he overwhelmed the Egyptians at the Red Sea — points forward to the NT's final judgment. Christ, who came first as the Lamb, comes finally as the Judge: “the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God” (2 Thessalonians 1:7-8). The adversaries pursued into darkness is the NT's outer darkness (Matthew 22:13; Nahum 25:30): those who reject Christ are not merely defeated but excluded from the light that is Christ himself (John 8:12; Revelation 22:5).
9: A shadow: 'What do you plot against the LORD? He will make a complete end; trouble will not rise up a second time.' The futility of plotting against YHWH — who brings a complete and unrepeated end to his enemies — is the OT ground for the NT's confidence that the victory of the cross is permanent. Christ's resurrection is YHWH's complete end to sin and death: “Death is swallowed up in victory” (1 Corinthians 15:54). Trouble will not rise a second time: “Christ, being raised from the dead, will never die again; death no longer has dominion over him” (Romans 6:9). The complete end that Nahum promises against YHWH's adversaries is accomplished at the resurrection — the definitive end of the power of sin and death.
10: A shadow: 'For they are like entangled thorns, like drunkards as they drink; they are consumed like stubble fully dried.' The consuming fire of divine judgment — the wicked consumed as dry stubble — is the OT image Jesus draws on: the chaff burned with unquenchable fire (Matthew 3:12; Luke 3:17). John the Baptist announces that the coming one will clear his threshing floor, gathering wheat into barns but burning chaff with unquenchable fire — the Nahum-pattern of the wicked consumed like stubble brought to its eschatological fulfilment in Christ's judgment.
11: A shadow: 'From you came one who plotted evil against the LORD, a worthless counselor.' The evil counselor who plots against YHWH — Nineveh's royal advisors who planned Assyria's campaigns against Israel — points to the pattern of anti-God counsel that reaches its ultimate expression in the conspiracy against Jesus: “the kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers were gathered together, against the Lord and against his Anointed” (Acts 4:26, citing Psalms 2:2). The plotting against YHWH that Nahum condemns is the exact act of Caiaphas, Pilate, and the crowd — yet God turned their evil counsel into the means of salvation (Acts 4:27-28: they did what your hand and your plan had predestined to take place).
12: A revelation of God: ‘Thus says the LORD, “Though they are at full strength and many, they will be cut down and pass away. Though I have afflicted you, I will afflict you no more.”’ The divine word of comfort to Judah after Assyrian devastation is the pattern of the NT's assurance: though God's people pass through affliction, the affliction has a promised end. “For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us” (Romans 8:18). More directly: Christ's affliction is the basis for the end of affliction — “he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed” (Isaiah 53:5). The “I will afflict you no more” is the new covenant promise secured by Christ's having borne the affliction in our place.
13: A shadow: 'And now I will break his yoke from off you and will burst your bonds apart.' The breaking of the Assyrian yoke — YHWH's liberation of Israel from imperial bondage — is the OT liberation pattern that reaches its fullest expression in Christ's work: “For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery” (Galatians 5:1). The yoke Christ breaks is not political but spiritual: the yoke of sin, law as condemnation, and death (Romans 8:2: the law of the Spirit of life has set you free in Christ Jesus from the law of sin and death). Every political liberation in the OT — from Egypt, from Assyria, from Babylon — is the shadow of the ultimate liberation Christ achieves.
14: A shadow: ‘The LORD has given commandment about you: “No more shall your name be perpetuated; from the house of your gods I will cut off the carved image and the metal image. I will make your grave, for you are vile.”’ The divine decree that Nineveh's name and gods be cut off is the pattern of Revelation's fall-of-Babylon oracles (Revelation 18:21-23: the great city will be thrown down with violence, and will be found no more). The idols cut off from their temples anticipate the complete dethronement of all false gods before Christ: “at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord” (Philippians 2:10-11). The idols that demanded worship are silenced; Christ alone receives the worship that belongs to God.
15: A shadow: 'Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace! Keep your feasts, O Judah; fulfill your vows, for never again shall the worthless pass through you; he is utterly cut off.' The messenger of good news whose feet appear on the mountains is the OT's most concentrated image of gospel proclamation. Isaiah 52:7 takes up the same image for the post-exilic return, and Paul cites Isaiah in Romans 10:15: “How beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news!” for the proclamation of the gospel of Christ. Nahum's good news is the fall of Nineveh — YHWH has acted to liberate his people; the gospel's good news is the fall of every power that oppresses humanity (sin, death, the devil) — YHWH has acted in Christ to liberate all who trust him. The messenger's feet on the mountains in Nahum become the apostle's feet in Romans become the Lamb's victory song in Revelation (Revelation 5:9-10).
